steps.title: Find what the text is doing
steps.content: The text introduces Ellison and notes that influential figures have championed his work. It then focuses on Harold Blake's afterword to Little, Big, explaining exactly why Blake praises the novel: its "adroit blend" of the naive and sentimental modes. The text exists to relay Blake's reason for admiring that specific novel.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘To argue that all writing must be classified as belonging to one of two categories’: The text describes Schiller's two categories but never argues all writing must fit them — in fact it says Little, Big defies that split. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘To compare the work of a writer with the work of a poet who admired him’: Owen Mercer is mentioned only in passing; no comparison of Ellison's and Mercer's works is made. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘To explain what inspired an author to write a particular work’: Nothing in the text discusses what inspired Ellison to write anything. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘To present a reason why a literary critic is impressed by a certain novel’: The text explains that Blake (a literary critic) praises Little, Big because it blends the naive and sentimental modes. That is exactly a reason a critic is impressed by a novel. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
